sections.plain_summary: IgG food sensitivities are delayed immune reactions to specific foods, where your body produces IgG antibodies against food proteins. Unlike food allergies (which cause immediate reactions), these sensitivities typically trigger symptoms hours or even days after eating, making the culprit food harder to identify. Common symptoms include bloating, fatigue, headaches, and joint pain. Management focuses on identifying trigger foods through testing or elimination diets and then avoiding or rotating them.
sections.detailed_summary: IgG food sensitivities represent a distinct type of adverse food reaction involving IgG antibodies—a common antibody class in the immune system. When you eat a food you are sensitive to, your immune system produces IgG antibodies against it, potentially triggering inflammation and symptoms in your digestive tract, joints, skin, brain, and other organs. The reaction is dose-dependent and delayed, usually appearing 4–72 hours after exposure, which makes it difficult to connect symptoms to specific foods by observation alone. It differs from true food allergies (IgE-mediated, immediate) and from food intolerances (lacking immune involvement). The prevalence and clinical significance of IgG food sensitivities remain debated in mainstream medicine, but many people report improvement after identifying and avoiding their trigger foods. Current approaches include clinical testing (IgG panels), empirical elimination diets, and rotation diets to reduce immune load and allow healing.

prevalence.summary: Affects a significant portion of the population, though exact numbers are uncertain due to debate over clinical significance.
prevalence.estimate: 10–30% of adults report food sensitivity symptoms; however, the clinical relevance of positive IgG tests is contested
prevalence.notes: IgG antibodies to foods are common in healthy people and do not always cause symptoms. Estimates vary widely depending on testing method and symptom criteria. Some mainstream physicians question whether IgG sensitivities are a distinct clinical entity, while functional and integrative practitioners report high prevalence. Symptoms may be more common in people with irritable bowel syndrome, autoimmune conditions, or leaky gut.

subgroups.name: Digestive-predominant sensitivities
subgroups.description: Primary symptoms are confined to the gastrointestinal tract, with bloating, gas, diarrhea, or constipation as main complaints.
distinguishing_features: Symptoms appear within 4–24 hours of consuming trigger food
Often overlap with irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) symptoms
May be driven by dysbiosis or intestinal permeability

subgroups.name: Systemic sensitivities
subgroups.description: Reactions affect multiple organ systems simultaneously—skin, joints, brain, and immune system—beyond just digestion.
distinguishing_features: Delayed symptom onset (24–72 hours) is more pronounced
May include fatigue, brain fog, joint pain, or eczema flares
Often associated with chronic inflammation or autoimmune conditions

subgroups.name: Cumulative-load sensitivities
subgroups.description: Reactions depend on total intake of the trigger food over time rather than a single exposure, making symptoms unpredictable.
distinguishing_features: Consuming small amounts may cause no reaction, but repeated or large amounts trigger symptoms
Symptoms may build over days of exposure
May respond well to rotation diets rather than complete avoidance

symptoms.notes: Often appears 2–24 hours after eating a trigger food; may persist for hours or days
Delayed onset (4–48 hours) is typical; distinguishes from lactose intolerance or other rapid-onset reactions
Some people experience constipation rather than diarrhea; inflammation may affect gut motility
Often accompanied by visible bloating and abdominal discomfort
May appear 12–48 hours after exposure; sometimes profound enough to restrict activities
Often reported alongside fatigue; may improve within days of removing trigger food
Delayed-onset headaches (12–48 hours after eating) are characteristic; may be severe
Typically appears 24–72 hours after exposure; may affect multiple joints; sometimes mistaken for arthritis
Often delayed 24–48 hours; itching may be severe; may worsen existing eczema or dermatitis
Persistent or worsening congestion appearing within 24–48 hours
May develop 1–3 days after eating trigger food; slow to heal
Often overlooked; may improve when trigger foods are removed
Can occur 12–48 hours after exposure; inflammation drives fluid retention

key_patterns: A symptom (bloating, headache, fatigue, joint pain) that appears consistently 4–72 hours after eating certain foods, then improves when that food is avoided for several days
Multiple symptoms that worsen together in response to the same foods—e.g., bloating + fatigue + brain fog after wheat
Symptoms that improve significantly during a strict elimination phase (1–2 weeks), then return when foods are reintroduced one at a time
A strong family history of autoimmune disease, allergies, or digestive problems; IgG sensitivities often cluster in families
Symptoms that overlap with IBS, eczema, or mild arthritis but do not fully respond to standard treatments

at_home.name: Food and symptom diary
at_home.description: Record everything you eat and drink (with approximate portion sizes and ingredients if possible) and note any symptoms that appear within 72 hours. Use a simple table or app (e.g., Notes, Notion, MyFitnessPal) with columns for date, time, food/drink, and symptoms. After 2–4 weeks, review for patterns: do the same foods appear before the same symptoms? Do symptoms improve on days you avoid certain foods? This is low-cost and practical, and it reveals patterns specific to your body.
at_home.safety_notes: Diaries are non-invasive and safe, but they rely on your memory and perception of timing. Very delayed reactions (beyond 72 hours) are easy to miss. If symptoms are severe or you suspect a true food allergy (immediate, severe reactions), stop food trials and see a doctor instead.

at_home.name: Structured elimination diet (2-phase)
at_home.description: Phase 1 (elimination): Remove your top 5–7 suspect foods for 1–2 weeks strictly. Choose suspects based on your diary or common triggers (wheat, dairy, eggs, soy, corn, nuts, sugar). Eat only whole foods you are confident about. Keep a daily symptom score (0–10 scale for overall severity). Phase 2 (reintroduction): On day 15, add back one suspect food, eat it 2–3 times over 3 days, and watch for symptom return. If symptoms return 12–72 hours after eating the food, it is likely a trigger. Wait until symptoms resolve, then test the next food. This is inexpensive and reveals which specific foods are problematic.
at_home.safety_notes: Safe for most people, but if you have severe malnutrition, an eating disorder, or a history of food allergy anaphylaxis, work with a clinician. Do not eliminate essential food groups without professional guidance. If symptoms worsen during elimination, stop and consult a doctor. Reintroduction should be done one food at a time with 3–7 days between tests to avoid confusion.

at_home.name: Symptom tracking with food photographs
at_home.description: Photograph everything you eat and drink before consuming it, then note the time and any symptoms that appear in the following 72 hours. Include portion context (e.g., size of plate, number of bites). After 2–3 weeks, review the photos and symptom timeline to identify patterns. This adds visual clarity and makes it easier to spot ingredient overlaps (e.g., all trigger meals contained corn).
at_home.safety_notes: Safe and can improve accuracy of food recall. Not a diagnostic test, but a useful complement to a diary.

clinical.name: IgG food-specific antibody panel
clinical.description: Blood test that measures IgG antibodies against 50–200+ specific foods. You provide a small blood sample (via finger prick or venipuncture), and the lab uses ELISA or similar immunoassay to quantify IgG levels to each food. Results are typically reported as negative, borderline, or positive, sometimes with concentration levels. Interpretation varies: some labs and practitioners treat any positive result as clinically significant, while others note that IgG antibodies are normal and present even in asymptomatic people.
clinical.availability: Widely available through functional medicine clinics, naturopaths, online health platforms, and some integrative MDs. Does not require a hospital referral in most regions. Cost is typically $200–$500 USD out-of-pocket; not usually covered by insurance. Common testing companies include ALCAT, Immun4, Everlywell, and others. Results usually available in 1–2 weeks.

clinical.name: Elimination diet supervised by a registered dietitian
clinical.description: A structured, professionally guided elimination diet lasting 4–8 weeks. A dietitian designs a personalized elimination phase (typically removing 4–8 common triggers based on your symptoms and history), monitors your nutrition and symptom response, and then guides a controlled reintroduction phase one food at a time. The dietitian ensures you are not missing key nutrients and helps you interpret symptom patterns. This is more rigorous and personalized than self-directed elimination.
clinical.availability: Available through gastroenterologists, integrative medicine clinics, functional medicine doctors, or private dietitian practices. Some insurance plans cover dietitian visits if referred by a physician; others require out-of-pocket payment ($100–$300 per session). Usually takes 4–8 weeks of regular check-ins. Particularly useful if symptoms are complex or nutrition is a concern.

clinical.name: Intestinal permeability test (leaky gut marker)
clinical.description: Blood or urine test that measures zonulin (a protein regulating intestinal tight junctions) or the ratio of lactulose to mannitol absorption. The theory is that IgG sensitivities may result from or be worsened by increased intestinal permeability, and testing for it can guide targeted treatment. You consume a solution of sugar molecules, then urine is collected to measure absorption ratios. Elevated zonulin or abnormal lactulose/mannitol ratios suggest 'leaky gut.'
clinical.availability: Available through functional medicine and integrative practitioners; not mainstream. Cost: $200–$400 USD. Clinical significance is still debated—leaky gut is not recognized as a disease diagnosis by conventional gastroenterology, though intestinal permeability does vary and may play a role in immune activation. Useful as part of a broader picture, not as a standalone diagnostic.

clinical.name: Microbiome stool test
clinical.description: Stool test measuring bacterial composition, diversity, and dysbiosis markers. The rationale is that dysbiosis (imbalanced gut bacteria) can drive intestinal permeability and IgG responses. Test results show dominant species, beneficial bacteria levels, and imbalance markers. Interpretation and treatment recommendations vary widely between labs.
clinical.availability: Available through functional medicine clinics and online platforms (Ombre, Everlywell, Thorne, etc.). Cost: $200–$400 USD. Not covered by insurance. Results and recommendations vary by lab; professional interpretation is advised.

clinical.name: Allergy skin prick test (SPT) or serum-specific IgE test
clinical.description: These tests diagnose true IgE-mediated food allergies, not IgG sensitivities. Skin prick test applies allergen extracts to the skin and observes for a wheal reaction; serum-specific IgE measures IgE antibodies in blood. These are relevant if you suspect true food allergies (immediate reactions: hives, throat tightness, anaphylaxis) rather than delayed sensitivities. Important to rule out true allergy before assuming IgG sensitivity.
clinical.availability: Ordered by allergists or primary care doctors. Widely covered by insurance. Available at most hospitals and specialty clinics. Results available within 1–2 weeks.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: IgG food sensitivities and IBS often co-occur. Many people diagnosed with IBS report that identifying and avoiding IgG-trigger foods reduces symptoms significantly. IBS may predispose to increased intestinal permeability and IgG responses, or IgG sensitivities may be a root cause of IBS-like symptoms in some cases.

related_conditions.name: Leaky gut syndrome (increased intestinal permeability)
related_conditions.explanation: Increased intestinal permeability may allow larger food molecules and bacterial antigens to cross the intestinal barrier, triggering IgG antibody production. Some practitioners believe IgG sensitivities are a consequence of leaky gut, making gut healing a key part of recovery.

related_conditions.name: Eczema and atopic dermatitis
related_conditions.explanation: People with eczema often have elevated IgG antibodies to foods and report skin flares after eating trigger foods. IgG food sensitivities may drive or worsen atopic inflammation in the skin.

related_conditions.name: Migraine headaches
related_conditions.explanation: IgG sensitivities are a recognized dietary trigger for migraines in some individuals. Delayed-onset migraines (12–48 hours after eating a trigger food) are particularly suggestive of IgG sensitivity.

related_conditions.name: Rheumatoid arthritis and other autoimmune joint diseases
related_conditions.explanation: Food sensitivities, including IgG responses, can trigger or exacerbate joint inflammation. Some people with autoimmune arthritis report that removing trigger foods reduces pain and swelling. IgG sensitivities may be a modifiable driver of autoimmune flares.

related_conditions.name: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and behavioral problems in children
related_conditions.explanation: Some parents and practitioners report that IgG food sensitivities (particularly to common allergens like dairy, wheat, or additives) worsen ADHD symptoms or behavioral issues in children. Evidence is mixed, but elimination diets have shown benefit in some cases.

related_conditions.name: Chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS)
related_conditions.explanation: People with ME/CFS often report worsening fatigue after certain foods, consistent with IgG sensitivity. Food sensitivities may contribute to post-exertional malaise or inflammatory flares.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Both histamine intolerance and IgG sensitivities cause delayed, multi-system symptoms (bloating, fatigue, headaches). However, histamine intolerance is dose-dependent on histamine content of specific foods (aged cheeses, fermented foods, leftovers), whereas IgG sensitivities are protein-specific. Testing and management differ.

related_conditions.name: Food intolerances (e.g., lactose intolerance, fructose malabsorption)
related_conditions.explanation: Both IgG sensitivities and food intolerances cause digestive symptoms, but intolerances lack immune involvement. Intolerances are usually dose-dependent and improve with enzyme replacement or reduced intake. IgG sensitivities trigger immune responses and may cause systemic symptoms. Diagnosis and management differ.

related_conditions.name: Celiac disease and non-celiac gluten sensitivity (NCGS)
related_conditions.explanation: Celiac disease is an autoimmune response to gluten (mediated by tissue transglutaminase antibodies); NCGS involves non-immune mechanisms. IgG food sensitivities to wheat are distinct from both. However, people with celiac disease or NCGS may also have IgG sensitivities to other foods. Testing and management should address both possibilities.

strategies.name: Avoidance of identified trigger foods
strategies.description: Once you identify a trigger food (via testing or elimination diet), avoid it completely for 4–12 weeks while your gut heals and inflammation subsides. This is the most direct way to stop symptoms. After a healing period, some people can reintroduce the food in small amounts or rotate it (eating it only once every 4+ days) without triggering symptoms.
strategies.notes: Most effective when combined with gut-healing interventions. Individual response varies: some people can tolerate reintroduction after 3 months; others need permanent avoidance. Cross-reactivity is possible (e.g., sensitivity to one grain may mean sensitivity to others). Work with a dietitian to ensure you are not creating nutritional gaps.

strategies.name: Elimination and rotation diet
strategies.description: After identifying trigger foods, remove them for 4–8 weeks, then reintroduce them on a rotation schedule (e.g., eating wheat only once every 4–5 days). This approach reduces cumulative antigen load and may allow tolerance to develop. Some people find they can tolerate trigger foods in small amounts or at low frequency without symptoms returning.
strategies.notes: Rotation diets work better for cumulative-load sensitivities than strict sensitivities. Requires careful planning and tracking. May not work for all foods; some remain problematic even on rotation.

strategies.name: Gut healing and sealing ('leaky gut' repair)
strategies.description: Consume foods and supplements that support intestinal barrier function: bone broth (collagen, gelatin), L-glutamine (an amino acid that rebuilds intestinal lining), zinc, vitamin A, omega-3 fatty acids, and soluble fiber (psyllium, slippery elm). The theory is that healing intestinal permeability reduces future IgG responses. Avoid foods and habits that damage the barrier (ultra-processed foods, excess alcohol, NSAIDs, chronic stress).
strategies.notes: Evidence for specific supplements is limited, but bone broth and L-glutamine have anecdotal support. No large clinical trials prove that gut sealing reverses IgG sensitivities, but reducing permeability is biologically plausible. This is most effective when combined with avoidance of triggers and anti-inflammatory diet.

strategies.name: Anti-inflammatory diet (e.g., Mediterranean, whole-foods-based)
strategies.description: Eat whole foods with minimal processing: fresh vegetables, fruits, fish, legumes, nuts, seeds, and healthy oils. Minimize refined sugars, ultra-processed foods, seed oils, and excess salt. This baseline diet reduces overall immune activation and inflammation, creating a more favorable environment for healing. It also makes it easier to isolate specific food triggers.
strategies.notes: No single diet works for everyone; Mediterranean and whole-foods-based diets have strong evidence for reducing inflammation. Avoid any diet that eliminates entire food groups without professional guidance.

strategies.name: Digestive enzyme supplementation
strategies.description: Take broad-spectrum digestive enzymes (protease, amylase, lipase) with meals to improve food breakdown and reduce large, incompletely digested protein particles that may trigger IgG responses. Some people also use enzymes like peptidase or bromelain specifically to degrade proteins. The theory is that better digestion reduces antigen load and IgG activation.
strategies.notes: Evidence for effectiveness is limited. May help if digestion is weak, but is not a substitute for avoidance. Safe for most people, but those with pancreatic disease or specific allergies should check with a doctor first.

strategies.name: Bone broth or collagen supplementation
strategies.description: Consume bone broth (homemade or commercial) or collagen powder daily (10–20 g) to provide amino acids (collagen, gelatin, proline, glycine) that support intestinal lining repair and immune tolerance. Bone broth also provides minerals (magnesium, calcium) and may have mild anti-inflammatory effects via amino acids like glutamine.
strategies.notes: Popular in functional medicine, but limited clinical trial evidence. Safe and nutrient-dense. May work best when combined with strict trigger food avoidance and gut healing overall.

strategies.name: L-glutamine supplementation
strategies.description: Take L-glutamine (5–20 g daily, divided doses) to support intestinal epithelial cell repair and reduce permeability. Glutamine is the primary fuel for intestinal cells. Dosing and duration vary; typical protocols last 4–12 weeks.
strategies.notes: Some clinical evidence supports L-glutamine for irritable bowel syndrome and post-surgical gut healing. Less evidence for IgG sensitivities specifically, but mechanistically plausible. Generally well-tolerated; mild bloating is possible at high doses. Avoid if you have a glutamine synthetase deficiency (rare).

strategies.name: Zinc and vitamin A supplementation
strategies.description: Zinc and vitamin A support intestinal barrier function, immune regulation, and mucosal healing. Typical doses: zinc 15–30 mg daily (as glycinate for absorption), vitamin A 5,000–10,000 IU daily. Both are essential for immune tolerance and barrier integrity.
strategies.notes: Evidence is stronger for zinc in wound healing and immune function. Vitamin A is critical for mucosal immunity. Do not exceed recommended doses, especially vitamin A (which is fat-soluble and can accumulate). Work with a practitioner to determine appropriate dosing for your needs.

strategies.name: Probiotics and fermented foods
strategies.description: Consume fermented foods (sauerkraut, kimchi, miso, tempeh, kefir) or take a multi-strain probiotic supplement (20–50 billion CFU daily) to promote a healthy, diverse microbiota. A balanced microbiota may reduce intestinal permeability and lower IgG activation. Fermented foods also provide short-chain fatty acids (butyrate, propionate) that strengthen the intestinal barrier.
strategies.notes: Probiotic effectiveness is strain-specific and varies by individual. Some people tolerate fermented foods poorly initially due to FODMAP content or histamines. Start with small amounts and increase gradually. Choose multi-strain, well-researched products (e.g., Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium species).

strategies.name: Stress management and sleep optimization
strategies.description: Chronic stress and poor sleep impair intestinal barrier function and increase immune activation, worsening IgG sensitivity symptoms. Prioritize 7–9 hours of sleep nightly, manage stress with meditation, yoga, breathing exercises, or therapy, and maintain a regular sleep schedule. Stress management alone won't cure sensitivities, but it removes a major barrier to healing.
strategies.notes: Sleep deprivation and chronic stress directly increase intestinal permeability and inflammatory markers. These are foundational to any healing protocol. Even modest improvements (1–2 extra hours of sleep, 10 minutes daily meditation) can reduce symptom severity.

strategies.name: Regular movement and exercise (low to moderate intensity)
strategies.description: Regular, gentle exercise (walking, swimming, yoga, cycling) improves digestion, reduces inflammation, and supports immune regulation. Avoid intense exercise or overtraining during acute symptom flares, as they can worsen inflammation. Aim for 30 minutes of moderate activity most days.
strategies.notes: Overtraining or exercising during symptom flares can exacerbate inflammation and immune activation. Rest days and recovery are important. Gentle movement is more helpful during active symptom phases than intense training.

strategies.name: Hydration and electrolyte balance
strategies.description: Drink plenty of filtered water (half your body weight in ounces is a rough guideline) and ensure adequate electrolytes (sodium, potassium, magnesium). Dehydration and electrolyte imbalances worsen inflammation and impair gut barrier function. Some people benefit from adding mineral-rich sea salt to water or drinking electrolyte-balanced beverages.
strategies.notes: Hydration supports detoxification and nutrient absorption. Electrolyte balance is especially important if you have diarrhea or high sweat loss. Avoid excess plain water without minerals, which can deplete electrolytes.

strategies.name: Antihistamine foods and low-histamine diet (if concurrent histamine intolerance)
strategies.description: If you suspect concurrent histamine intolerance, choose fresh, recently cooked foods and avoid aged, fermented, and processed foods high in histamine. Include histamine-degrading herbs and foods (ginger, turmeric, fresh herbs). Some people benefit from a low-histamine trial to see if it reduces symptoms alongside trigger food avoidance.
strategies.notes: Histamine intolerance and IgG sensitivities can co-occur. A low-histamine diet trial may help you identify if histamine sensitivity is compounding your symptoms. However, many fermented foods (sauerkraut, kefir) are both beneficial probiotics and high-histamine, so careful sequencing is needed.

strategies.name: Reduce FODMAP intake during acute healing
strategies.description: FODMAPs (fermentable carbohydrates) can worsen bloating and gas in people with dysbiosis or leaky gut. A low-FODMAP diet for 4–8 weeks may reduce digestive symptoms while your gut heals. After healing, gradually reintroduce FODMAPs to restore bacterial diversity.
strategies.notes: Low-FODMAP is not a long-term diet; it is a temporary elimination to reduce symptom load and allow healing. Prolonged restriction can reduce bacterial diversity. Work with a dietitian experienced in both low-FODMAP and IgG sensitivities to avoid nutritional gaps.

things_to_avoid: Continuing to eat identified trigger foods—even small amounts can perpetuate inflammation and prevent healing. Avoidance is the foundation of management.
Ultra-processed foods and artificial additives (colors, flavors, preservatives)—these can trigger or worsen immune responses independently and make it harder to identify true IgG triggers.
Excess sugar and refined carbohydrates—these feed dysbiotic bacteria and increase intestinal permeability, worsening IgG responses and inflammation.
High-dose NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) taken regularly—these damage the intestinal barrier and increase permeability, directly undermining healing efforts.
Excessive alcohol—damages intestinal barrier function and increases inflammation and permeability.
Chronic high stress without management—stress hormones increase intestinal permeability and impair immune regulation. Stress management is foundational to healing.
Sleep deprivation—profound barrier disruption and immune dysfunction result from chronic poor sleep. Prioritize 7–9 hours nightly.
Overtraining or intense exercise during acute symptom phases—can exacerbate inflammation and delay healing. Gentle movement is better during flares.
Assuming IgG sensitivities explain all symptoms without ruling out true food allergies, celiac disease, or other conditions—testing and professional evaluation ensure accurate diagnosis.
Eliminating entire food groups permanently without professional guidance—nutritional gaps can develop, and some people can eventually tolerate reintroduction with proper healing.

key_levers.lever: Identify and eliminate trigger foods (via testing or elimination diet)
key_levers.why_it_matters: This removes the primary driver of inflammation and IgG activation. Without this step, other interventions have limited effect. Symptoms often improve dramatically once triggers are identified and consistently avoided.
key_levers.how_to_start: Begin a 2-week food and symptom diary, or if you are ready to commit, start a structured 2-week elimination diet removing your top 5 suspects (wheat, dairy, soy, eggs, corn). Track symptom changes daily.
key_levers.timeline: Symptom improvements often appear within 3–7 days of eliminating triggers, though full improvement may take 2–4 weeks as inflammation subsides.

key_levers.lever: Heal intestinal permeability and support the gut barrier
key_levers.why_it_matters: Increased intestinal permeability is thought to underlie or perpetuate IgG sensitivities. Supporting barrier function with targeted nutrition, supplements, and anti-inflammatory diet reduces future IgG activation and can allow eventual tolerance of previously problematic foods.
key_levers.how_to_start: Add bone broth (homemade or high-quality store-bought), L-glutamine (5 g twice daily), or collagen powder to daily routine. Simultaneously commit to a whole-foods anti-inflammatory diet. Start with one supplement and add others gradually to assess tolerance.
key_levers.timeline: Intestinal healing typically takes 4–12 weeks. You may notice reduced symptom severity within 2–4 weeks and be able to tolerate some previously problematic foods after 8–12 weeks of healing.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Dysbiosis (imbalanced gut bacteria) can drive intestinal permeability and increase IgG responses. A diverse, balanced microbiota strengthens the barrier, produces short-chain fatty acids that support intestinal health, and reduces inflammation.
key_levers.how_to_start: Consume 1–2 servings of fermented foods daily (sauerkraut, miso, tempeh—choose based on your tolerance), or take a multi-strain probiotic (20–50 billion CFU). Increase soluble fiber intake slowly (psyllium, flax, oats if tolerated) to feed beneficial bacteria.
key_levers.timeline: Microbiota shifts can be detected within 2–4 weeks; clinical improvements may appear within 4–8 weeks. Sustained improvement usually requires ongoing probiotic and fermented food consumption.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Sleep deprivation and chronic stress directly increase intestinal permeability and immune activation, undoing other healing efforts. Sleep is foundational to barrier repair and immune regulation. Even modest improvements dramatically reduce symptom flares.
key_levers.how_to_start: Commit to a consistent sleep schedule (same bedtime and wake time daily), aim for 7–9 hours nightly, and establish a 30-minute wind-down routine (no screens, dim lights, meditation or reading). Add 5–10 minutes of daily stress management (meditation, deep breathing, or gentle yoga).
key_levers.timeline: Sleep improvements can reduce symptom severity within 1–2 weeks. Deeper improvements in immune tolerance develop over 4–8 weeks of consistent quality sleep and stress reduction.

key_levers.lever: Work with a functional or integrative medicine practitioner for personalized testing and protocol
key_levers.why_it_matters: Professional guidance accelerates diagnosis, prevents nutritional gaps, adjusts supplements based on your specific profile, and addresses comorbid conditions (e.g., dysbiosis, low stomach acid, parasites) that may be perpetuating sensitivities.
key_levers.how_to_start: Seek a functional medicine doctor, registered dietitian, or integrative medicine practitioner experienced in food sensitivities. Request IgG testing or a supervised elimination diet, and ask for a comprehensive gut healing protocol tailored to your needs.
key_levers.timeline: Professional protocols typically last 8–16 weeks. Symptom improvement often appears within 4 weeks; sustained improvement and food reintroduction take 12+ weeks.

common_mistakes: Eliminating too many foods at once—makes it impossible to identify true triggers and increases risk of nutritional deficiency and disordered eating patterns. Eliminate 3–5 suspects at a time, not 20.

Reintroducing trigger foods too quickly—symptoms may reappear 12–72 hours after reintroduction, but if you introduce a new food every day, you won't be able to identify which one caused the reaction. Reintroduce one food every 3–5 days.

Relying on IgG testing alone without symptom correlation—positive IgG tests are common in healthy people. Use testing as a guide, but confirm with elimination diet and symptom response. The best diagnosis combines testing and clinical correlation.

Avoiding trigger foods for too long without also healing the gut—avoidance buys time, but without addressing intestinal permeability, dysbiosis, or other drivers, you may develop sensitivities to new foods over time. Combine avoidance with active healing.

Skipping the healing phase and jumping straight to reintroduction—if you reintroduce trigger foods before your gut has healed (usually 8–12 weeks), inflammation flares and you may develop new sensitivities. Patience during the healing phase pays off.

Not addressing underlying dysbiosis or low stomach acid—if dysbiosis or hypochlorhydria is present, healing efforts will be slow or incomplete. Consider testing and targeted support (probiotics, L-glutamine, digestive enzymes, or supplemental HCl) for these drivers.

Creating nutritional deficiencies through overly restrictive elimination—avoid eliminating entire food groups (all grains, all legumes) unless testing clearly indicates widespread sensitivity. Work with a dietitian to ensure adequate nutrition.

Neglecting sleep and stress—these undermine all other efforts. Without sleep and stress management, intestinal permeability remains high and immune activation continues. Prioritize these foundational elements.

Overtraining during the healing phase—intense exercise during acute symptom flares and early healing increases inflammation and delays recovery. Gentle movement is better; save intense training for after symptoms have resolved.

Assuming IgG sensitivities are the only problem—comorbid conditions (celiac disease, SIBO, food-borne infections, parasites, nutrient deficiencies) may coexist and need separate treatment. Professional evaluation ensures comprehensive diagnosis.

specialists.role: Evaluates IgG sensitivities within the context of overall health and gut function. Orders comprehensive testing (IgG panels, microbiome, intestinal permeability), designs personalized elimination and healing protocols, and monitors progress. Combines conventional medical knowledge with functional medicine approaches.
specialists.notes: More likely than conventional doctors to take IgG sensitivities seriously. Requires out-of-pocket cost for visits and specialized testing in many regions. Look for practitioners with additional training in clinical nutrition or functional medicine (AAFM, IFM certified).

specialists.type: Registered Dietitian (RD or RDN), especially those trained in functional or medical nutrition therapy
specialists.role: Designs safe, nutritionally complete elimination and healing diets tailored to your symptoms and test results. Monitors nutrient status, guides reintroduction phases, and adjusts protocols based on response. Ensures you don't develop deficiencies. Often the most practical first step.
specialists.notes: Insurance may cover dietitian visits if referred by a physician. Look for credentials: RD or RDN (registered), ideally with additional training in gastroenterology or functional nutrition. Many private practitioners offer consultations without a physician referral.

specialists.type: Gastroenterologist with interest in functional or integrative medicine
specialists.role: Can rule out structural GI disease (IBD, celiac, parasites) and support diagnosis of IgG sensitivities. May order specialized testing and offer professional credibility in discussions with other providers. Some gastroenterologists dismiss IgG sensitivities; seek those with integrative leanings.
specialists.notes: Conventional gastroenterologists may be skeptical of IgG sensitivities, but some have incorporated functional approaches. Ask directly whether they recognize and treat IgG food sensitivities.

specialists.role: Specializes in dietary and botanical approaches to food sensitivities and gut healing. Often experienced with elimination diets, supplements, and herbal protocols. In some U.S. states, NDs are licensed; in others, they operate without regulation.
specialists.notes: Variable credibility and regulation by region. Choose licensed NDs (in regulated states) or those with additional credentials from recognized programs (AANP, Bastyr, etc.). Be cautious of practitioners recommending very restrictive diets or aggressive supplement regimens without a clear rationale.

specialists.type: Integrative or Functional Medicine Nurse Practitioner (NP) or Physician Assistant (PA)
specialists.role: Provide functional medicine approaches, order testing, and design protocols under physician supervision. Often more accessible and affordable than MDs. Can manage ongoing care and adjust protocols.
specialists.notes: Credentials vary; look for NPs and PAs with additional training in functional medicine (AAFM certified) or clinical nutrition. May be part of a functional medicine clinic or private practice.

specialists.type: Acupuncturist with training in dietary therapy
specialists.role: Some acupuncturists have training in Traditional Chinese Medicine dietary principles and food energetics. May complement elimination diet work and help manage stress and immune function.
specialists.notes: Useful as an adjunct to other interventions, not a primary diagnostic tool for IgG sensitivities. Ensure the practitioner has recognized certification (LAc, Dipl. Ac.) and is willing to coordinate with your other healthcare providers.

patient_communities.name: IgG Food Sensitivities Support Group (Facebook)
patient_communities.notes: Multiple active groups where people share test results, symptom experiences, and healing strategies. Useful for finding real-world examples and emotional support, but medical advice should be verified by a professional. Moderated groups are more reliable.

patient_communities.name: r/IBS, r/FODMAP, r/CuteLesions (skin-focused) subreddits
patient_communities.notes: Subreddits where people discuss food sensitivities, elimination diets, and symptom management. Unmoderated; advice varies in quality. Useful for finding elimination diet tips and shared experiences, but always verify medical advice with a professional.

patient_communities.name: FODMAP and Digestive Health Support Communities
patient_communities.notes: Smaller communities focused on digestive health and low-FODMAP diets, which often overlap with IgG sensitivity management. May offer more curated information and active moderation than larger platforms.

patient_communities.name: Functional Medicine Coaching Programs (e.g., Institute for Functional Medicine, Cleveland Clinic Center for Functional Medicine)
patient_communities.url: https://www.ifm.org/find-a-practitioner/ or local university integrative medicine centers
patient_communities.notes: Professional directories where you can find certified functional medicine practitioners, coaches, and dietitians. More reliable than unmoderated peer communities; practitioners are vetted.

patient_communities.name: Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics Dietitian Locator
patient_communities.notes: Official directory of registered dietitians. Filter by specialization (gastroenterology, functional nutrition) to find professionals focused on food sensitivities and gut healing.

learning_resources.title: The Elimination Diet: Discover Which Foods Are Making You Sick
learning_resources.description: A practical guide to conducting a structured elimination diet with detailed instructions for each phase (elimination, reintroduction, maintenance). Includes meal plans, recipes, and symptom tracking tools. Focused on food sensitivities including IgG reactions.

learning_resources.title: The Gut Psychology Syndrome (GAPS) Diet
learning_resources.description: Details a healing protocol emphasizing bone broth, fermented foods, and nutrient-dense whole foods to repair gut permeability and reduce food sensitivities. Covers the theory of 'leaky gut' and its role in food sensitivities and systemic symptoms.

learning_resources.title: IBS: Free Forever (or equivalent elimination diet resources)
learning_resources.description: Online courses and guides specifically addressing IBS and food sensitivities using elimination diet approaches. Often include video instruction, meal plans, and shopping lists.

learning_resources.title: The Functional Medicine Approach to Gastrointestinal Health
learning_resources.description: Comprehensive educational resources on functional approaches to GI health, including food sensitivities, dysbiosis, and intestinal permeability. Professional-level content but some resources are publicly available.

learning_resources.title: Why Zebras Don't Get Ulcers (Stress and Health)
learning_resources.description: Explains the mechanism by which chronic stress impairs intestinal barrier function and immune regulation. Helps contextualize why stress management is foundational to food sensitivity recovery.

learning_resources.title: The Autoimmune Paleo Cookbook & The Paleo Answer
learning_resources.description: Resources focused on paleo and autoimmune protocols, which often align with elimination diet principles used for IgG sensitivities. Includes recipes eliminating common inflammatory foods.

learning_resources.title: Mind-Gut Connection (stress, sleep, and digestive health)
learning_resources.description: Explores the brain-gut axis and how stress, sleep, and psychological factors influence digestive health and food sensitivity severity. Explains why stress management and sleep are foundational.

learning_resources.title: Low-FODMAP Dietitian Resources
learning_resources.description: Evidence-based information on low-FODMAP diets, which overlap with food sensitivity management. Useful for understanding how fermentable carbohydrates can worsen symptoms during healing phases.

learning_resources.title: Cleveland Clinic Center for Functional Medicine Resources
learning_resources.description: Articles and patient education from a reputable academic medical center incorporating functional medicine approaches to food sensitivities and gut health.

learning_resources.title: PubMed and Google Scholar searches on 'IgG food sensitivities' and 'intestinal permeability'
learning_resources.description: For those interested in peer-reviewed evidence, these databases contain research on IgG responses to foods, dysbiosis, and intestinal barrier function. Searchable and often have free abstracts.

learning_resources.title: The Second Brain / Gut Feelings (podcast series on gut-brain health)
learning_resources.description: Podcasts exploring the gut-brain axis, stress, and immune function. Help contextualize why psychological and sleep interventions are so important for food sensitivity recovery.

sections.relevance_signal: **Delayed reactions 4–72 hours after eating** — hard to link to a specific food. Symptoms include bloating, fatigue, skin issues, and brain fog with no clear immediate trigger.
